architecture.summary: The paper trains sparse autoencoders (SAEs) on the hidden representations of three single-cell foundation models (scGPT, scFoundation, Geneformer). The SAE learns a dictionary of interpretable features from the frozen model's activations. Information flows from the scFM's hidden states into the SAE encoder, which produces sparse feature activations that can be decoded back. The learned features are then intervened upon (suppressed or activated) to steer model behavior, with interventions returning as modifications to the hidden state that propagate forward through the frozen scFM.

biblio.title: Sparse Autoencoders Reveal Interpretable Features in Single-Cell Foundation Models

claims.statement: SAE-learned features reveal diverse and complex biological and technical signals, which emerge even in pre-trained models
The encoding of information differs between scFMs with distinct training protocols and architectures
SAE-derived features are functionally related to model behavior and can be intervened upon
Suppressing batch-associated features reduces unwanted technical variation and improves data integration while preserving the core biological signal
Activating drug-encoding features steers control cells toward drug-perturbed states in a concentration-dependent manner
